topic: A neighbor asks to use part of the yard for a garden
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sunlight felt too bright, a harsh glare on the freshly tilled earth. I stood on the edge of my lawn, my hands gripping the weathered wooden fence post until my knuckles were white. When Mrs. Henderson approached, her smile was wide, almost too wide, and she spoke in a cheerful, rapid-fire stream about heirloom tomatoes and soil amendments. Every word she uttered seemed to land like a small stone in my chest, making it harder to draw a full breath. I could only manage a tight nod, my gaze fixed on the distant, overgrown rose bushes, wishing the ground would simply swallow me whole.

[story 2]

My stomach performed a slow, nauseating roll as I watched him circle the perimeter of my property, pointing at a patch of overgrown weeds. He had such an easy confidence, a casual air about borrowing space. When he finally laid out his plans—a sprawling patch of lavender and sunflowers—my throat tightened. I kept my hands clasped tightly behind my back, a small, involuntary tremor running through my arms. I tried to form a coherent sentence, something polite, but all that escaped was a thin, reedy sound. I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to bolt, to vanish into the dense hedge line.

[story 3]

I kept staring at the cracks in the pavement, tracing their patterns with my eyes as the neighbor explained his vision for the garden. His enthusiasm was infectious, loud and vibrant, and it grated against the quiet, fragile state I found myself in. I shifted my weight from one foot to the other, a restless, jerky motion. When he asked if I had any objections, the question felt like a spotlight. My mouth went dry. I swallowed hard, the movement visible in my neck, and managed a whisper that barely carried over the buzzing of a nearby insect—a sound so small it felt utterly inadequate to express the churning anxiety inside me.
